personality_voice.book_baseline: Book Moody is defined by paranoid vigilance ('CONSTANT VIGILANCE' as a repeated verbal tic), a gravelly growling voice, blunt directness, dark humor, and a willingness to operate outside rules when he judges it necessary. He is simultaneously intimidating and unexpectedly kind — he takes Neville aside after the Cruciatus demonstration, lends him a book, and notices Harry's distress. He is sardonic with authority figures (Karkaroff, Fudge, Snape), openly contemptuous of cowardice and disloyalty, and speaks in short, declarative sentences with occasional harsh laughs. He is also genuinely perceptive — he reads situations quickly, notices Harry under the Invisibility Cloak, spots Crouch on the Marauder's Map, and identifies Snape's arm-grab. His paranoia is presented as both a flaw and a professional asset.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the surface markers: the growling voice, the hip flask, the magical eye, 'CONSTANT VIGILANCE,' the ferret incident, the Unforgivable Curses lesson, and the general atmosphere of menace. However, the film Moody is almost entirely the impostor (Barty Crouch Jr.), and the corpus shows that the film collapses the distinction between the real Moody's personality and the impostor's performance. The real Moody appears only briefly in GoF (end scene, twitchy at the feast) and more substantially in OotP. In OotP the film preserves his gruffness, his operational command during the flight from Privet Drive, and his protectiveness of Harry. However, the quieter, more humane dimensions — the Neville kindness, the Marauder's Map conversation, the career advice, the sardonic wit in the Pensieve flashback — are absent.
personality_voice.difference: The film loses the interior texture of Moody's voice: the sardonic asides ('Another old friend,' his commentary on Karkaroff in the Pensieve), the unexpected gentleness with Neville, the dry humor about his own paranoia, and the extended conversational register that shows him as a complex thinker rather than just a menacing presence. The 'CONSTANT VIGILANCE' catchphrase is preserved but the philosophical weight behind it — his extended classroom speeches about preparation, the nature of Dark magic, the Imperius Curse resistance lesson — is compressed. His speech in the film is almost entirely reactive commands and growls; the book's Moody also lectures, reasons aloud, and reveals his worldview.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core traits (paranoia, gruffness, vigilance, physical distinctiveness, dark authority) are preserved well enough that the character is recognizable. However, important secondary traits — unexpected warmth, sardonic wit, the reasoning mind behind the paranoia — are reduced. The film also conflates the impostor's performance with the real Moody's personality for most of GoF, which means the 'personality' on screen is partly Barty Crouch Jr.'s interpretation. Score sits in the 14-17 range: recognizable but with meaningful reduction of secondary traits and emotional register.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Book Moody serves multiple narrative functions across GoF and OotP: (1) Defense teacher who genuinely prepares students for Dark magic; (2) active investigator — he searches Snape's office, uses the Marauder's Map to hunt Crouch, confronts Snape on the stairs, and pieces together the conspiracy before anyone else; (3) protector of Harry — he guides Harry toward the Summoning Charm solution, covers for him on the stairs, and patrols the maze; (4) Order veteran who contextualizes the first war through the photograph and Pensieve scenes; (5) the dramatic irony engine — his apparent loyalty and competence make the reveal devastating. In OotP he commands the escort mission, briefs the Order, and is the operational leader of the guard.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The film preserves his role as Defense teacher (Unforgivable Curses lesson) and the ferret incident. In GoF, his investigative agency is almost entirely removed: the Marauder's Map scene is cut, the Snape confrontation on the stairs is cut, his piecing together of the conspiracy in the teachers' meeting is reduced to one line ('You seem to have given this a fair bit of thought, Mad-Eye' — present in the book but the film version of this scene is compressed). His guidance of Harry toward the Summoning Charm is retained but shortened. In OotP, his operational command of the escort is preserved and he is shown briefing the group, which is faithful. However, his role in the Department of Mysteries battle is minimal on screen — he appears and fights but has no distinct agency. The photograph/Order history scene is transferred to Sirius in the film.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The Marauder's Map scene (where Moody investigates Crouch, covers for Harry, and reveals his obsession with Death Eaters who walked free) is entirely cut. This is a major agency loss: in the book it is the scene that most clearly shows Moody as an active investigator and foreshadows the reveal. The Snape staircase confrontation is cut. His role as the person who most clearly identifies the conspiracy ('Someone put Potter's name in that goblet knowing he'd have to compete') is present in the film but compressed. The photograph scene — where he contextualizes the first war and shows emotional investment in the fallen — is given to Sirius.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: Multiple specific acts of agency are removed or transferred. The Marauder's Map scene is the single most important cut: it is where the real Moody's investigative mind is most visible and where the dramatic irony is sharpest (the impostor is hunting himself). The transfer of the photograph scene to Sirius removes Moody's function as living memory of the first Order. These are not mere compressions — they change what the character does in the story. Score falls into the 6-9 range.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: The real Moody's motivations are: genuine hatred of Dark wizards and those who escaped justice; loyalty to Dumbledore and the Order; a professional ethic that distinguishes him from Death Eaters ('he never killed if he could help it, always brought people in alive'); and a deep, almost obsessive investment in preparedness born from decades of trauma. His internal conflict is subtle: his paranoia is both his greatest strength and a liability that makes him easy to impersonate and isolate. He is also shown to have genuine affection for students like Neville and Harry, which sits in tension with his harsh pedagogical methods.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film establishes his vigilance and hatred of Dark magic through the classroom scenes and his operational behavior. His protectiveness of Harry is shown. However, his professional ethic (never killing when capture is possible) is never articulated. His emotional investment in the fallen Order members — which in the book motivates his obsessive surveillance of Snape and Karkaroff — is not shown in the film (the photograph scene is Sirius's). His motivation for being at Hogwarts (a favor to Dumbledore, one year only) is mentioned briefly in the classroom scene and is present.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The film does not articulate the distinction between Moody's methods and Death Eater methods, which in the book is a key part of his moral identity. His obsession with Death Eaters who walked free — the motivation that makes the Barty Crouch Jr. reveal so ironic — is not established for the real Moody in the film (it appears only in the impostor's confession). The emotional weight of his investment in the first war's casualties is transferred to Sirius.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Motivations are partially present (vigilance, loyalty to Dumbledore, protectiveness of Harry) but the deeper layer — the moral code that distinguishes him, the obsession with justice for escaped Death Eaters, the trauma-driven paranoia — is underexplained. This is a meaningful flattening, not just compression. Score in the 8-10 range.

character_arc.book_baseline: Moody's arc across the series is relatively flat in terms of internal change — he is consistently the battle-scarred, paranoid veteran — but has a structural arc: introduced as a legend (GoF pre-Hogwarts), revealed as an impostor (GoF climax), restored as the real person (GoF end, OotP), and killed in action (DH). The arc is about the gap between reputation and reality, and ultimately about a man whose vigilance was not enough to save him. His death in DH is framed as a genuine loss — 'the consummate survivor' who could not survive. The real Moody's brief appearances after the reveal (twitchy at the feast, OotP escort, DH Seven Potters) show a man shaken but still operational.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film follows the same structural arc: legend → impostor reveal → real Moody in OotP → death in DH. The GoF end scene (real Moody twitchy at the feast) is present. OotP shows him operational. DH shows his death reported by Bill. The arc is compressed but structurally intact.
character_arc.difference: The film compresses the restoration arc — the real Moody's brief GoF appearance is very short, and his OotP presence, while present, omits the photograph scene and the quieter moments that show him processing his imprisonment. His death in DH is handled faithfully (reported off-screen, mourned, toast raised). The arc is present but some of the texture of the post-reveal recovery is missing.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc is structurally faithful and the key beats are present. Compression is noticeable but does not fundamentally alter the trajectory. Score in the 11-13 range.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Moody's key relationships: (1) Dumbledore — deep mutual trust, Moody defers to him but also challenges him ('Alastor!' as a warning); (2) Harry — genuine investment in Harry's survival and potential, expressed through the Summoning Charm hint, the career advice, the Marauder's Map scene, and the OotP escort; (3) Snape — open contempt and suspicion, active surveillance, the staircase confrontation is a key scene; (4) Neville — unexpected tenderness, the post-lesson kindness is one of the most humanizing moments for Moody; (5) the Order — he is a founding member, his photograph contextualizes his place in the community of the fallen.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Dumbledore relationship is present but reduced to brief exchanges. The Harry relationship is shown through the classroom, the Summoning Charm hint, and the OotP escort — partially faithful. The Snape relationship is almost entirely absent: the staircase confrontation is cut, and Snape's wariness of Moody (a key dynamic in the book) is not shown. The Neville relationship is reduced to a brief post-lesson moment without the book's depth (no lending of the book, no extended kindness). The Order relationship loses the photograph scene.
key_relationships.difference: The Snape-Moody dynamic is a significant loss: in the book it establishes Moody as the one person Snape is visibly afraid of, and it drives the subplot about who is surveilling whom. The Neville relationship loses its most humanizing beat. The Order history relationship is transferred to Sirius.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: Two of Moody's five key relationships are substantially weakened (Snape, Neville) and one is transferred (Order history to Sirius). The Harry and Dumbledore relationships are partially preserved. Score in the 5-6 range.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Book Moody's complexity comes from: the tension between his harshness and his unexpected kindness (Neville); his sardonic self-awareness about his own paranoia ('they say old Mad-Eye's obsessed... but I'm nothing compared to Barty Crouch'); his moral code that distinguishes him from the people he hunts; the dramatic irony of the impostor being more 'Moody-like' in some ways than the real man; his role as living memory of the first war; the Marauder's Map scene where his investigative mind is fully on display; and the OotP photograph scene where his grief for the fallen is visible.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves the surface complexity (the eye, the leg, the paranoia, the gruffness) but loses most of the interior complexity. The Neville kindness is reduced. The sardonic self-awareness is absent. The moral code is not articulated. The Marauder's Map scene is cut entirely. The photograph scene is transferred. What remains is a vivid but somewhat one-dimensional figure: the scary, paranoid Auror.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The film loses the material that made Moody more than his surface: the unexpected warmth, the self-aware humor, the moral distinctions, the investigative intelligence shown in extended scenes, and the emotional weight of his connection to the first war's dead. These are not incidental — they are what prevent him from being merely a grotesque.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The character is reduced to a simpler function: intimidating veteran/protector. The contradictions (harsh but kind, paranoid but perceptive, rule-breaking but principled) are mostly lost. Score in the 5-7 range.

lost_or_transferred_material: Marauder's Map scene (GoF): Moody investigates Crouch, covers for Harry, reveals obsession with Death Eaters who walked free — entirely cut
Snape staircase confrontation (GoF): Moody actively surveils and intimidates Snape, Snape's fear of Moody established — cut
Post-Cruciatus lesson kindness to Neville: lending the Herbology book, the gentler register — reduced to a brief moment
Imperius Curse resistance lesson: Moody puts each student under the curse, Harry's resistance is a key scene — cut
Career advice to Harry ('You ever thought of a career as an Auror?') — cut
Moody's sardonic commentary in the Pensieve flashback (Karkaroff trial) — compressed, most asides removed
Original Order of the Phoenix photograph scene — transferred to Sirius
Moody's articulated moral code: 'he never killed if he could help it, always brought people in alive' — not present in film
Moody's self-aware humor about his own paranoia — absent
Snape's visible wariness/fear of Moody — not established in film
Moody's use of the Marauder's Map to search for Crouch in the forest — cut
Moody's Secrecy Sensor/Dark Detector office scene and extended conversation with Harry — cut
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 (score above 75 if core book functions/agency transferred to another character): photograph/Order history scene transferred to Sirius
Cap 1 (score above 85 if films remove major complexity/agency): multiple complexity and agency losses confirmed
comparative.key_observations: The film preserves Moody's iconic surface — the magical eye, the wooden leg, the hip flask, the growling voice, 'CONSTANT VIGILANCE,' the ferret incident, the Unforgivable Curses lesson — well enough that he is immediately recognizable. However, the adaptation loses almost everything that made him complex beneath that surface. The two most damaging cuts are the Marauder's Map scene (his single greatest moment of active agency and investigative intelligence in GoF, and the scene that most sharpens the dramatic irony of the impostor) and the Snape staircase confrontation (which established the only relationship in which Snape was visibly afraid). The transfer of the Order photograph scene to Sirius removes Moody's function as living memory of the first war. The Neville kindness is reduced to a gesture. What remains is a vivid but flattened figure: the scary, paranoid veteran. The film also faces a structural problem unique to this character — for most of GoF, the 'Moody' on screen is Barty Crouch Jr.'s performance, and the film does not clearly distinguish between the impostor's exaggerated version of Moody and the real man's more nuanced personality. The real Moody's brief post-reveal appearances are too short to compensate. The total score of 55 reflects a character who is recognizable and partially faithful in personality and arc, but substantially diminished in agency, relationships, motivation depth, and complexity.
